desc:
The monotony of the forest is broken by an extremely dense grove of pines. Their
knotted trunks are mottled in brown, and their thin scraggly branches plum out
with greyish green needles. The air smells strongly of sap and there is a red
bed of needles lying on the forest floor, mixing in with the moist rich earth
where many light green ferns grow in the dim light.
name: Thicket of Pine

desc:
Sending ripples and gurgles along the water surface, the river Entwash flows
from the west and down into the south. It swiftly moves across and around
pebbles and stones until it meets a giant boulder that sits in the middle of the
river, where it splits to pass on either side. The limestone bank consists of
roots that slither among the giant boles and rocks in the misty light.
name: Bend in the Entwash

desc:
The river flows from the north to the east. It flows over pebbles and stones,
and along the banks, sending ripples across the water's surface. A bank composed
of worn limestone rises a few feet into the air, where row upon row of strong
roots dangle. At the top of the forest lies a tight blanket of woven branches
and leaves that connect into a wall of grey trees in the mist.
name: River Entwash

desc:
The Entwash river gracefully meanders south. Its progress is smoother and less
lively than where it descended from the spring at the foot of the Misty
Mountains. Its water is crystal clear and looks refreshing and wholesome. The
rich vegetation to either side of the river seems testament to the water's
qualities, at least for plant-kind.
name: A Graceful Bend in the Entwash

desc:
The forest is dominated by evergreen trees, primarily firs and spruces, yet they
do not grow so thickly as to make the air unpleasantly stale. In fact, this
region of the forest feels decidedly fresher and more open in comparison to the
area east and south where the vegetation grows denser and darker.
name: Among the Evergreens

desc:
The forest looms threateningly around any outsiders that dare travel here,
creating a dark pocket, often unnerving all but the bravest or most foolhardy.
The closely packed firs and spruces make travelling difficult as roots and twigs
seem to grasp and trip at limbs with uncanny purpose. Things seem brighter to
the west and north however. Perhaps progress would be easier in those
directions.
name: An Airless Part of the Forest

desc:
Deep inside this part of the forest, firs and spruces abound, clustered so
densely together that little light reaches the forest floor. Yet the trees do
not seem to be particularly malicious here, even though the air is rather stuffy
from their considerable density. To the north the forest seems more open.
Eastward there seems to be an opening of some kind.
name: Within a Dark Fir Forest

desc:
Where the ground is too rocky to allow growth as thick as in most other areas,
the trees seem to have been forced to grow further apart. Indeed the air
currents above the canopy can reach down to rustle the leaves of the lower
branches and inject an uncommon freshness to this place. With the oppressive
airlessness gone, this forest seems almost inviting and friendly, albeit still
somewhat watchful.
name: A Small Glade

desc:
The ground rises sharply from west to east, and is not easily negotiated. As
well as the uneven rocks, roots and low scrubby plants between the trees make
going particularly tough. Whether climbing up or down the slope, or attempting
to traverse it north or southwards, travellers face almost equal challenges. The
forest seems to open into a small glade west of here.
name: On a Steep Rocky Slope

desc:
To the west a treacherous and rocky slope falls sharply away. The trees, densely
packed seemingly everywhere, do not thin out even on so steep a gradient. In all
directions, shabby old oaks and horse chestnuts dominate, covered in blankets of
lichen and moss. A deep littering of dried leaves and twigs covers everything as
if they have not been disturbed for hundreds of years.
name: At the Top of a Steep Slope

desc:
Unexpectedly, the ground drops gradually away into a round depression about
two-score yards from side to side. The dense line of trees around the edge stops
quite suddenly on the lip of the dingle and green grass takes over. The bowl
itself is devoid of all larger growth and there are no paths or tracks of any
sort, though the grass is long and rampant. If this place was used for some
purpose in the past it looks to have been abandoned many years ago.
name: Within a Small Dingle

desc:
The ground slopes gently from north and west to south and east. It is not
particularly steep, but among the thickly grouped trunks of oak and beech is a
small rocky outcrop, from which cool clear spring water tinkles. It splashes
between some worn and moss-covered rocks before sinking slowly between root and
stone out of sight.
name: At a Small Spring

desc:
Despite being so crowded, the elderly beech trees that cram together on each
side seem to fit so neatly together that they hardly jostle each other at all.
Indeed, the trees look comfortable together in a way that only the aged-old
strangely sentient trees of Fangorn Forest can.
name: Crowded Beeches

desc:
The forest here seems to be almost entirely made of broad-leaved trees. They are
packed in so tightly that movement to the east and south seems impossible. Yet
there is still enough light coming down from the thick canopy above to provide
for dense brambly undergrowth as well. Spongy masses of moss and lichen cling to
every ancient surface.
name: Within a Dense Deciduous Forest

desc:
It is scarcely possible to move among the trees, with every step involving a
forced struggle past twisting grasping tendrils and branches. Every twig seems
to pull, scrape and scratch, hindering movement as effectively as a giant
spider's web. Indeed, further progress east and north is entirely impractical
from here.
name: Among Densely Packed Trees

desc:
Why the trees crowd so thickly in this area is a mystery. Perhaps the soil is
particularly good here, or water is plentiful, or perhaps the reason is
something beyond mortal comprehension. Yet it is clearly impossible to forge a
path further east at this point. The forest seems no less dense north or south
either, though to the west the trees thin out considerably.
name: Within the Depths of the Forest

desc:
The forest grows so thickly here that there is scarcely enough room for one
trunk to widen and grow between its neighbours. One can only imagine the tangled
battle going on with all the roots beneath the ground. The way eastwards and
southwards is entirely impassable, with scarcely an easier path north, though
mercifully the trees thin out a good deal to the west.
name: Struggling Forest between the Trees

desc:
Stretching from west to north, this seems to be a surprisingly open and airy
swathe through Fangorn Forest. The trees, mainly fir and pine, are spread wide
as if this is an avenue sometimes travelled upon. There is no actual path
discernible however, so care must still be taken to not stumble on twisted roots
or half-buried stones.
name: Along an Open Stretch of Forest

desc:
The pine trees thin out rather suddenly, surrounding an almost perfectly round
area of soft grass. They seem to be keeping their distance almost reverentially
as if in respect and awe of the home of a great elder. An easily passable area
cuts south from this clearing, seeming almost like an intentional path.
name: At a Great Clearing

desc:
It is a reflection of the unpredictable and dynamic nature of the land of
Fangorn that even the very earth seems somewhat aware. There appears to be a
struggle between the oaks and beeches and the coniferous trees, yet a sandy
break in the earth has caused the two forces to remain separate. The strip of
land is so dry not even the deepest roots of the most ancient broadleaves are
able to penetrate it and encroach on the evergreens on the southern side.
name: At an Arid Sandy Break

desc:
A change is taking place here. The broad-leaved oaks and beeches to the north
are giving way to the pines and firs of the west and south. If it is a battle
between the deciduous trees and the conifers it is much too slow a conflict for
the hasty folk to see. Nevertheless, there is a feeling of animosity and tension
here, but one that seems to be entirely between the trees themselves.
name: Between the Battling Trees

desc:
The ground is covered with a deep carpet of moss, quite soft to the touch. The
trees that are standing here are dim coloured and their branches are strung with
enormous grey-green leaves and stringy mosses upon their boles. There is a faint
trickling sound in the westward direction, hinting towards a source of water.
name: Forest by the Entwash

desc:
The forest seems thickly and oppressively crowded here, giving the air a half
choked and stifling feel. The effect seems to worsen as one travels eastward. An
elderly rowan tree stands from the rest slightly, its limbs pointing southwards
as if to gesture away from the darkest groves.
name: By the Rowan Tree

desc:
Grey looming pine trees press densely here, keeping out not only all light but
all other vegetation as well. They seem to lean inwards maliciously to block and
hinder anyone who dares travel here so much so that movement in any direction
other than back westward is impossible.
name: At a Dark Knot of Pine Trees

desc:
Across what little passage there is through the forest lies a massive and
ancient fallen tree. Curiously, the remaining trees seem gathered around it,
leaning over as if lamenting the loss of a dear old friend. It is remarkable
that none of them were crushed when the old tree fell, if indeed they were in
their current positions when it did so.
name: By the Ancient Fallen Tree

desc:
The land slopes gently upwards to form a low hill here, and a reasonable view of
the surroundings may be gained from the top of it. Scores upon scores of trees
lie on every side, though there does seem to be a clear glade a little to the
north. Westwards, the forest appears much darker and more impenetrable.
name: Upon a Small Rise

desc:
The ground is rough and uneven here, with a tangle of rocks and roots that
hamper the growth of many plants. The earth rises to the west and also to the
north where the east-west slope steepens. No terrain seems too tough, however,
for the mix of old and shabby oaks and ashes that dominate this part of Fangorn
Forest.
name: Hilly Ground

desc:
The land slopes away in all directions from this point. Although there is no
clear view out of the forest from this rise, the air is a little fresher here
than in the thick depths of the forest. The vegetation gives way at the very top
of the hill to a mossy and lichen-covered outcrop, giving the hilltop a rather
bald look.
name: At a Worn Mossy Outcrop

desc:
The forest crowds densely around, preventing fresh air from reaching anything
living at ground level. Even with the lack of wind there is often a low howling
sound coming through the trees. A tall, old and entirely hollow pine stands here
and perhaps the wind is entering the top of its trunk, causing an eerie
reverberation down its length, like a huge living pipe organ.
name: The Old Hollow Pine

desc:
Coniferous trees abound in this area, clustering close together. They give the
impression of protecting each other as if safety in numbers is a priority,
rather than merely growing together because of space constraints. Whatever it is
that can cow an entire grove of evergreen trees must be dangerous indeed.
name: Within a Grove of Conifers

desc:
The pines that grow in this part of Fangorn are particularly tall, shaggy and
bear the signs of age. Their grey bark, when not obscured by moss and lichen, is
gnarled and peeling. Yet the place has a clean and fresh aura to it, enhanced
greatly by the cliff face to the west. Its smooth rock face serves to reflect
fresh air down into the trees at its base.
name: The Cliff Face

desc:
The air is noticeably still and oppressive, with oaks and ashes looming close,
creating a confining atmosphere. The huge trees block out virtually all sound,
light and breeze. The way west is blocked by a huge tangle of thorns. Southwards
things darken even further, leading into an ominously black grove.
name: Among the Oaks and Ashes

desc:
The area is dominated by a dense tangle of hawthorns and blackberry brambles.
One specimen in particular is so large, ancient and twisted that it is hard to
identify anything other than impenetrable sharp twigs and spines. The way south
is completely blocked and it would be a struggle clearing a path eastwards.
name: A Tangle of Bushes

desc:
The densely packed pines, though tight here, give way considerably to the north
where the small hill climbs upwards to rise a little above the rest of the land.
To the west and south the ground seems flatter, though with the trees as closely
spaced as they are, walking would not be easy in either direction.
name: On a Slight Hill

desc:
Despite the shallowness of the soil and the barrenness of the frequent rocky
outcrops, pines and hemlocks manage to grip the earth and flourish as thickly as
anywhere else. Walking is tedious though, with both stones and roots aplenty to
turn a misplaced ankle.
name: On Rocky Ground

desc:
This area of the forest is one of great contrast. To the west and south the land
opens up and widely spaced oaks and beeches dominate the earth. To the north and
east, however, lie denser packed pines and firs blocking light and hindering the
growth of any shorter vegetation.
name: Within a Transition

desc:
Majestic oak trees soar over this area. Despite being large and well-spread,
their fronds let plenty of light through, allowing for an undergrowth rich in
tall grasses. The ground is slightly moist and boggy which no doubt aids the
grasses' growth. Insects flit from leaf to frond, filling the air with a quiet
humming.
name: In the Long Grass

desc:
A docile air permeates this region of Fangorn Forest, where elegant beech trees
rustle and sway on the bank of the river Entwash, as if greeting those who pass.
The beech trees all arch high and gracefully over the river, one sending out a
great bough that almost reaches the opposite bank.
name: At a Coppice of Beech Trees

desc:
The larger trees give way to ferns and reeds as the ground slopes gently down
towards the Entwash river south of here. The air is light and airy, a welcome
relief from the oppressive stuffiness between the trees. Those observant of
their surrounds might often feel that they are being watched from all
directions.
name: Marshy Ground near the Entwash

desc:
The vegetation is dense at all levels, as each plant struggles for supremacy
over its neighbour. Pines and beeches combat within the high canopy while bushes
of rosehip and brambles smother each other and the ground-covering grasses and
ferns below. However, the earth of Fangorn is a match for them all, somehow
providing sustenance to each and every one.
name: Dense Vegetation

desc:
This is not a pleasant part of Fangorn Forest. It is altogether silent and
ominous. North of here a vicious tangle of thorns and briars bars the way as
thoroughly as any artificial wall, though south and east of here, passage may be
made through the dense and tangled undergrowth to lighter parts of the forest.
To the east lies a frighteningly dark grove.
name: At the Edge of a Black Grove

desc:
The trees mysteriously shuffle to block the way out for any intruders entering
this most evil and perilous place, leaving a narrow escape northwards only. No
outside light penetrates this grove at all, and an intense feeling of malice and
hatred to all the outside world permeates everything.
name: In the Black Grove

desc:
The evergreen-rich forest continues in all directions apart from west where a
large dense tangle of branches bars the way completely. It is impossible to see
beyond the massive living wall, let alone pass through it. The trees must either
be protecting something or blocking something, but whatever the reason, that
barrier is most effective.
name: At a Dense Wall of Greenery

desc:
North and west of this area, dense vegetation makes travel very cumbersome. Yet
the passage south of here, through this swathe of forest and on eastwards is a
great deal easier. There is no set path to follow but the way is much clearer
than normal for this part of Fangorn, as it someone or something passes through
from time to time.
name: A Lighter Passage through the Trees

desc:
Ancient white and black spruces grow here at the foot of the mountain, hidden
below a large cliff. Their tops flat and spreading, their foliage blue, and nipt
with cold, as if centuries ago they had ceased growing upward against the bleak
sky. Their smooth bark is peeling off in small patches and countless lichen hang
in festoons from their lower branches, as testimonies of their great age. While
south the forest seems quite airless, east and west of here, spruce trees are to
be found.
name: Elder Spruces

desc:
The forest seems thinner, when compared to the forest east of here, showing that
it is coming to an end. Long tree-clad slopes rise from the lip of the dingle.
Beyond them, above the fir trees of the furthest ridge rises the peak of a high
mountain, sharp and white. On its sides, numerous crags prevent passage. Only
firs and mountain goats can be seen there. However, a path of yellow green
mountain grass seems to be outlined, up north among the firs, while east of
here, ancient white and black spruce trees can be found.
name: Spruce Dingle

desc:
As the earthy soil becomes more sparse, trees get scattered and firs replace the
less sturdy spruces. High up, sheer crags of rough limestone rocks form the only
passage along a tortuous curve. In many spots, the path only leads to a dead
end. A particular bluff leads to a place where a lonely cloud-flower grows. Its
strong star-like cluster of woolly silvery-gray leaves holds out firmly against
the mountain wind. Travelling eastwards from here, leads to a dusty trail
bordered north by wind carved stone pillars, while the way north leads up to a
fir plateau. Below to the south, the forest is quite thin, while clinging on to
the slopes of the mountain.
name: Silverstar Path

desc:
A narrow and shadowy breach between two wind-carved stone pillars seems to be
the only passage upwards. In front of it, a dusty pass runs straight, from east
to west, following the edge of a sheer rocky brink south, and surrounded north
by the cliff walls. From this overhang the higher-altitude evergreen forest
seems welcoming and hearty as the trees are more sparse than in the deeper
woods. Looking more carefully, one might remark on the absence of any visible
wildlife among the trees.
name: Breach Passage

desc:
A succession of tall stone slab steps makes a wretched path, probably used only
by nimble mountain goats or long legged giants. As the path reaches a plateau,
the ground lowers into a grassy theatre bordered by tiers of bulky rock seats.
In the middle of the place grows what should be the tallest fir of this land.
Its dark green fans, full of purple cones, extend almost to the border of the
grass, as if it were trying to protect the tiny young fir-trees below. While the
way southwards leads down a silverstar path, the way east takes one to a gusty
plateau, covered with limestone boulders.
name: Tall Fir Plateau

desc:
The walk to the ridge is fairly steep in places and it may be quite a difficult
hike among the pebbles dislodged from the mountain slope. However, above the
last ledge of rocks, the ground flattens a bit, and the footing changes to green
meadows carpeted with a dazzling variety of wild-flowers. Alpine poppy, sea
pink, and saxifrages add their bright red and purple colours to the shades of
green and grey of the forested mountains. Heading south leads to an elevated
plateau of firs, while going eastwards brings one to a cliff brink.
name: Last Ledge

desc:
The foot of the western summit ends here abruptly. North and east a sheer brink
leads to an upsetting precipice of black shining rock, some hundred feet above
the world of sharp boulder heaps beneath. This is a great lookout point to a
vista of rolling, wooded mountains and valleys. Some of the higher elevations
appear to be permanently covered in snow, almost elsewhere, treetops march down
the slopes towards the plains, as far as the eye can see.
name: High Ridge

desc:
A huge white limestone cliff beetles over the valley, forbidding any passage to
the south and west. Along the base of the cliff, overhanging a scree leading
down and north, a bulge provides shelter from the frequent rock slides and the
cold howling and shrieking wind. Under the bulge, a small dusty trail runs
leading to a wide grassy ledge jutting from the cliff face east of here.
name: Base of a Cliff

desc:
This dangerous scree seems to be the only passage between this side of the
glacial valley and the mountains above. The difficulty of the walk across the
sharp edged and wobbly stones is increased by the frequent avalanches of stones
from the nearby summits. Loose stones roll down, as if thrown by raging giants,
in a deafening din. Their frantic fall lasts until they clash against a bigger
rock or disappear into the valley below.
name: Dangerous Scree

desc:
This lateral moraine ridge marks the edge of a past glacier. Rock materials from
the valley walls were broken up by frost shattering and carried here by the
glacier as it followed its curve east along the valley. The resulting heap of
unconsolidated granite and limestone rocks makes travel difficult. From time to
time, loose rocks from the scree above roll crashing down.
name: Lateral Moraine

desc:
The glacier that once stretched along this trough is still visible on the slope
of the nearby summit. North of here, where the ice tongue starts, the last
seracs block the way further up along the valley. However, it appears possible
to climb a bit further northwards, walking cautiously on the ice, or south, on a
pile of moraine dragged there by the glacier. East, the glacial valley follows
its way down towards the Fangorn Forest.
name: Large Trough

desc:
The glacier ends here, enclosed between two abrupt mountain walls. The ice
tongue that covers the ground is not very thick, but quite slippery. The danger
however, comes from the impressive pinnacles of bluish ice hanging from above.
These seracs formed at the end of the icefall, where the glacier's surface
suddenly drops down, torn by many sets of crevices, block any passage further
north.
name: Ice Tongue

desc:
The snow from the mountain dome to the north, extends down to here, covering
patches of dried mountain grass. A few yellow tufts emerge here and there on the
slope, shaken by ascending winds. In the opposite direction, a glacial valley
can be seen down below. It is as if it is a field of bitter battle, between the
retreating ice tongue of the glacier and the climbing pines of Fangorn, slowed
down only by the harshness of the rocky soil.
name: Fringes of the Snow Field

desc:
Only a few stunted spiky green pine trees grow here on the earthy lines.
Everywhere else, the valley is cluttered with grey and brown rock fragments.
From the moraine barrier to the north, a trickle of water slips gently into a
small basin, where a few leeches wave harmlessly. The water then disappears back
under the rocks. While north of here, the glacial valley, following the eastern
lateral moraine ridge, heads towards the heart of the mountain, to the south,
some thin pinewoods extends on the less rocky soil.
name: Cluttered Valley

desc:
South of here, the pines seem to have been stopped by the impassable barrier of
the last recessional moraine, which crosses right through the valley. This mound
of material, however, is only an obstacle for the trees as the walk is made
easier by the large flattened granite slabs. From the top of the mound, most of
the valley is visible, from the pine hollow to the slopes of the sharp horn to
where the glacier has retreated. Heading south leads to a cluttered part of the
valley, while west brings one through a large trough. East, and upwards, a goat
track runs up across grass covered slopes and passes.
name: Glacial Valley

desc:
A succession of grassy hollows, full of young pines, descends into larger woods
to the east towards the heart of Fangorn. North, as the valley rises, enclosed
between the mountains faces, pines disappear quickly. And the moss- covered
rocks are replaced by cracked granite slabs and huge marble boulders, leftovers
from a now withdrawn glacier. West and south, cliffs of grey rocks, rising from
the depths of the earth, block any passage.
name: Thin Pinewoods

desc:
Starting from the base of the western cliff is a ledge, a few feet wide,
extending towards the east, bordered north by a sheer precipice and abutting
south in a steep scree. The easternmost part of the ledge rises against the
green waves of the forest, like the prow of a majestic vessel. From there, the
view is outstanding and colourful. Different shades of green, lighter in the
mountain pastures, darker and at times deep in the woods, mix with the grey of
the rocks that become white where the snow remains. Heading down towards the
south takes one to the base of a small scree, on a pile of rocks threatening to
fall further down, whereas to the west, the ledge leads to the base of a huge
cliff from the nearby summits.
name: Narrow Ledge

desc:
Large and smaller rocks have slid from the cliffs above into this breach and
below into the forest. Only the most skilled climber would be able to climb down
from here, and the sharp edges of the fallen rocks lying below at the base of
the cliff make it an unpleasant place to fall. The travel eastward could be
accomplished by jumping and clinging to one of the tree branches, as some of
them are hanging only a few feet away from the cliff wall. The passage south and
west is blocked by the rising cliff walls, yet it seems possible to walk up in a
steep scree, and reach a few feet wide ledge north of here.
name: A Rockslide

desc:
The scenery of green sods and gray rocks suddenly stops as it reaches the brink
of a very steep escarpment dominating the valley. From there an immense country
of uninterrupted forest stretches away up the Anduin Vale toward the east and
north. The trees spread over the least dell, leaving only a few bare bare rock
places. Even on the cliff walls, stubborn stunted firs manage to grow on the
slightest shelf of earth, firmly thrusting their roots into narrow cracks.
Somewhat to the south of here, a gusty plateau stretches out, while to the west
it seems possible to follow the cliff brink further.
name: Cliff Brink

desc:
Sloping just slightly, this elevated plateau is covered with piled up round
limestone boulders, lying over the edges of treacherous swallow holes. The
ground is worn away by the flowing spring water, yet on the thin earth layer, a
bright green bed of grass flourishes, sprinkled with deep blue gentian patches.
And under the shadow of the rocks, sheltered from the gusty wind, rosettes of
leathery plants with fragrant yellow flowers grow. East of here, a lonely tree
clings on to the edge of a cliff, which brink seems to run north of here.
Heading west leads to an elevated fir plateau, whereas below, a breach passage
spreads towards the south.
name: Gusty Plateau

desc:
Following the northern slope, an occasional warm ascending wind gently blows
through the fine thorns of the trees. The larch laces flutter softly, caressing
the spruces, motionless like concerned elders. These lush ornaments and the
quiet atmosphere greatly differs from the soberness of the forest's old
colonnades of deciduous trees rising east from here, and leading deep into the
heart of Fangorn. In every other direction, various kinds of evergreens fill the
landscape, larches in the harsh northern terrain, spruces in the west and firs
in the dark south.
name: Warm Groves

desc:
Upright in their strength, with their dainty boughs standing black on the white
sky, the beeches of this forest seem to hold firm against the oak colonnades,
with shaggy foliage coming from the depths of the forest. Motionless, the trees
seem to take part in a solemn and endless palaver. The damp, leaf-covered ground
muffles even the lowest sound, as if the trees did not want to be disturbed
during their private conversation. Except for the warm grove of spruces and
larches to the west, the surroundings are mainly populated with deciduous trees,
beeches lie to the north, oaks stand east, and south horse chestnuts border a
small glade.
name: Oak Colonnades

desc:
The oaks here are large, stately trees with thick trunks and wide irregular
canopies with glossy dark leaves. Their brownish-grey bark is highly striated
and devoid of any lichen, moss or parasites. Despite their odd awry aspect, the
trees do not have an unfriendly look, however, they seem to possess an ageless
wisdom, weird and awry, but ageless. From here, to the north, a mix of beeches
and oaks can be spotted, and the forest remains a mix of various deciduous tree
species in the other directions, as well.
name: Durmast Oaks

desc:
This part of the forest is a mix of deciduous species from the surroundings.
Beeches, birches, hornbeams and ashes grow scattered among chestnuts and oaks of
diverse kinds. Even between the same species, it seems impossible to find two
identical fellow trees or two identical leaves on the same tree. Even the
unlikely regular visitor of this place, after eons spent here, would still find
a new unexpected or unknown tree species. This strangely mixed forest extends a
bit more east from here, while in the other directions oaks seems to be the most
common kind of tree.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
When lit, this area of the forest reveals all sorts of trees from the
surroundings. Ashes, beeches, hornbeams and many more, are all easily found in
this place. The mixed forest in this area continues from a bit west of here. To
the east, ash trees dominate the forest, while to the north, the forest somehow
appears sadder than in other places. As a consequence of the variety of trees
growing here, all kinds of different leaves and branches of different feel and
make lie scattered, covering the ground here. The ground appears to suddenly
drop a little, into some kind of round depression south of here, forming a small
dingle.
name: Mixture of Different Tree Types

desc:
Medium-sized ash trees dominate this part of the forest, their bark firm and
brown-coloured with interlacing ridges, and their wood hard and strong. Even
though several sorts of ash trees can be found here, green ash trees appear to
be the dominant sort. Northwards from here, a rocky headland forms, with
hornbeams and taller ash trees than can be found here, while to the west, the
forest is a mixture of many different kinds of trees. To the east, ash trees
continue to be the prevailing sort of tree.
name: Green Ash Forest

desc:
The ash trees in this area mostly consist of the green and black sorts. The
green seeming to come in from the west, and the black from the east, gradually
fading into each other. When compared to the west, the bark of the trees appears
softer than before, easily reduced to some sort of powder if rubbed hard. But
even though the wood of these trees appears soft, it is quite durable and
sturdy. Both to the east and west of here, ash trees dominate the forest.
Northwards, however, a low-growing kind of plant with glossy leaves form a kind
of carpet on top of some rocky outcrop, while some crowded beeches lie to the
south of here.
name: Heart of the Ash Forest

desc:
The ash trees dominate the forest, both here, and to the north and west. The
footing is soft, padded with leaves and all other sorts of plant and tree debris
that have fallen over time. Passage is free in all directions but to the east.
In this direction, an extremely dense thicket renders any passage impossible. It
is also a bit more moist in this area than in other parts of the forest,
conditions the black type of ash trees prefers.
name: Black Ash Forest

desc:
Despite the small size of the thicket, it is dense enough to block out almost
all natural light. What little light makes it through is scattered by the leaves
above. No plant life survives these conditions. A few ants scurrying about are
all that can be recognised from normal forest wildlife. Heavy masses of vines
grow between the trees, preventing any travel south, but beyond them the forest
stretches on, deeper and more dense than here.
name: Tangled Thicket

desc:
A murmuring sound fills the air. No actual words can be discerned from the soft
muttering, but the quality of the noise resembles that of a strange language.
What little of the forest is visible through the twisted and disfigured limbs of
the trees growing here indicates that the surrounding area is just as dark. The
ground slopes away to the north seemingly leading into a small valley. To the
east and west the forest continues unbroken.
name: Whispering Woodland

desc:
The quiet enshrouding of the forest is broken by a high-pitched chattering,
seemingly emanating from the canopy above. While no birds can be seen, the
source of the sound remains somewhat of a mystery. To the north the trees grow
extremely close together, and a small gap between two of the more prominent
trees appears to lead into a dark tunnel. Further south the trees are just as
dense, an unbroken barrier to further travel.
name: Whispering Woodland

desc:
There is a distinct feeling that something is not right here, as if a great evil
reshaped this part of the forest and it is still suffering from it. It is very
dim and stuffy. Patches of darkness appear to twist and distort themselves when
looked at and the creaking sound of ancient trees blowing in the wind only adds
to the sense of unease.
name: Deep within an Eerie Wood

desc:
Any hope of venturing further south has to be abandoned in the face of the
foreboding wall of towering fir trees. Underneath the overhanging canopy
everything is damp and the unmistakable smell of rotting plant-matter lingers.
Some light enters the forest from the north, it would appear that the trees are
less dense there.
name: Towering Forest Wall

desc:
The green carpet of grass advancing into this area from the north is halted by a
line of fir trees. The grey hue of the trunks stands in stark contrast to the
deep green foliage spreading out from each tree, blocking out the sky. The
occasional fallen tree shows up deathly white against the background of brown
pine needles and cones. At the southernmost edge of the firs, an unnaturally
large number of dead trees lie in various stages of decay. Some great evil must
have changed this area of the forest long ago.
name: Approaching a Wall of Fir Trees

desc:
The trees here appear to shift slightly, perhaps it is just a trick of the
light. A closer look reveals that the trees are covered with a heavy blanket of
lichen giving them an almost hairy appearance. The only light to enter this
place comes from the north where a small clearing barely can be discerned
between the boughs. The southern boundary has become overpopulated, it would be
difficult to make much headway through the dense branches. The other directions
will surely prove to be easier routes.
name: Beneath a Canopy of Leaves

desc:
The ground here slopes away to the west. To the east a steep climb leads to the
top of a hill, however there is no respite from the thick forest surrounding as
the canopy continues unbroken. Gnarled and twisted trees are clustered together
on the south face of the slope creating an impenetrable barrier.
name: A Slope beneath the Canopy

desc:
Thick branches hanging low make movement arduous and painful. The long vines and
gnarled limbs protruding from the trees become hurtful obstacles on the way
through the forest. In every direction, there is more of the same gloomy forest.
To the south, piles of rocks between the trees make travel that way impossible.
name: On a Forested Hill

desc:
A solid wall of wood surrounds this grove on its northern and western
boundaries. Massive oak roots have grown there, knotting and twisting themselves
together to form a panel preventing further travel in those directions. A few
small gaps in the western wall allow a glimpse of what lies beyond, however,
they are small, and it is difficult to make out much detail.
name: At a Wall of Roots

desc:
An immense tree rises from amongst its smaller brethren dominating the thicket.
Its upper branches reaching through the living awning of leaves that sentence
the plants on the ground below to endure a perpetual gloom. To the east thin
shafts of light can be seen, the forest is less dense in that direction.
name: Thickly-wooded Knolls

desc:
The sombre, oppressive nature of the forest seems to be concentrated here. The
trees growing here are stunted, unable to reach their full height. Around the
base of each tree several thick roots have curled themselves, squeezing the
boughs as if trying to strangle the life from the oaks. The roots have become so
overgrown that they block movement in every direction, only the path to the west
remains passable. Despite their difficulties, the oaks have still managed to
develop a large system of branches shielding the forest floor from natural
light, and almost completely preventing fresh air from reaching this unpleasant
place, making the air stuffy and stale.
name: Impenetrable Roots

desc:
Everything here seems to be peaceful, even the ants that can be seen scurrying
around the base of a nearby oak, moving leaves to their home, seem to do so
silently. To the west the forest canopy becomes more open, allowing a small
amount of sunlight to reach the ground. A number of species of mushroom have
taken advantage of this and now grow over the stump of a long-dead tree. Heading
north would take travellers deeper into the woods but a mass of hanging vines
and branches make travel there impossible.
name: Border of a Leafy Canopy

desc:
The cool grass covering the ground here reaches around the boughs and stumps of
the oak trees which grow nearby. A cold, sharp draft can be felt and, listening
carefully, a dull murmuring can just about be made out above the noise of the
rustling leaves.
name: A Stand of Oaks

desc:
The trees here have grown particularly close together causing their branches to
become entangled. In certain areas, the forest appears to be completely
impenetrable, a wall of solid wood preventing progress. In order to make any
headway, travellers would be wise to choose their path with care.
name: Tangled Forest

desc:
Under the concealing canopy of the trees towering above, the moisture in the air
has been trapped resulting in a damp, boggy area forming between the trees.
Ferns have taken advantage of these conditions and seem to flourish. The swampy
terrain makes movement difficult.
name: Humid Forest

desc:
Lying among the trees, the fallen remains of a long dead rowan dominates the
surrounding area. Even after all this time, a large depression is still evident
where the tree's roots were pulled free as it fell. On the eastern side of the
trunk some stones and twigs have been collected and placed against the boughs
lying there.
name: At a Fallen Tree

desc:
Tall rowan trees have taken hold here. The ground is littered with decaying
leaves and a musty smell of decay hangs in the air. Above, the sky is almost
totally obscured by the intricate network of branches stretching between the
neighbouring trees.
name: Dark Area of the Forest

desc:
In the midst of this obscure and foggy forest, the trees are of a slate grey,
looking like inert stone pillars. Only a few grey leaves remain on their limbs,
and the ground, of an ash-like sand, is devoid of any debris. This forest seems
petrified, yet still alive and unwelcoming against any legged visitor, and it
will not allow any passage further north where nothing grow except dark grey
trees. Even the surrounding dark area of the forest seems to be a more
preferable place to visit.
name: Murky Petrified Forest

desc:
Here, the escarpment rim is dominated by hemlock but dotted by groves of maple,
birch, white ash, and red oak. Trees grow as far as the cliff edge to the east,
where the earthy ground ends abruptly in a mix of hanging moss and roots.
Nothing seems to point the brink of the cliff, and it would not be wise to run
aimlessly around, even less so close to the precipice. The forest extends south
of here following the abrupt end of the mountain, and rises in tiers on its
western slopes.
name: Forested Escarpment

desc:
The trees here are old and decrepit, their brown leaves turning to a ghostly
grey as they fall. Large boughs can be seen lying on the ground, rotten and
formless. The great silence of this deep forest girds the least unusual noise,
and only the wind dares to pass with its complaint, like a cry of hunger and
lassitude. Last vestige of greenness, ivy clings on the cliff wall to reach the
sunlight or maybe to escape from this dreary place. Beside, a large cleft
running up along the cliff towards the south, seems to be the surest mean of
leaving this place, as everywhere else the forest seems unfriendly, even
forbidding any passage north.
name: Decrepit Forest

desc:
The ground here is all covered with patches of low shrubs with wiry, angular
branches and leathery oval leaves. They rarely exceed a foot in height, but they
can hold as many berries as a full-sized bush. It seems impossible not to tread
on them, squeezing out of them their dark purple juice. Here and there a few
pines spring up, protecting the shrubs from the direct sunlight. But east, as
the ground falls rapidly, deciduous trees grow among the leaf-covered rocks.
South, the ground lowers, albeit gently, remaining grassy, while east, a bare
rock scenery extends from the foot of the mountain peaks.
name: Bilberry Fields

desc:
Dominating this wonderful and unusual world of cracks, a huge boulder forms a
natural gate that opens westwards to another valley. Here, the rock is of a
bright white hue and the scenery is open on most sides. To the west, beyond the
massive porch of greyish rock, the valley is dark and always charged with
electricity, as clouds bump and stick to the nearby summits. The mountain
renders passage north from here impossible, its side being much too steep and
treacherous to climb.
name: Boulder Gate

desc:
This valley is nestled between high cliffs on all sides, forming a magnificent
mountain cirque. The altitude of the mountains around adds a rather dark and
unfriendly atmosphere to this place. The only ingress or egress is through a
large natural gate, a few dozen feet wide and surmounted with a mighty
unreachable boulder. This huge rock, wedged between the cliff walls and covered
with a few wispy saplings, will probably fall down eventually, but not for some
time.
name: Mountain Cirque

desc:
Judging from the numerous tracks, this pass is used very often by mountain
goats, living in the rocky part of the mountain and grazing below where the
grass abounds. Northwest of here, an imposing snowy dome seems to serenely
dominate the area, while eastward, a seemingly endless view of sharp rocky peaks
surrounded by black clouds threaten the valley.
name: Green Pass

desc:
Rising a few hundred feet above the grassy pass to the west, this place is only
made of sharp rocks and small boulders torn off from the mountain walls. The
rough gneiss rock offers a sure hold. Walking along the edges between the peaks
should be easy, if one does not suffer from vertigo. On both sides, the view is
breathtaking, to the east the sheer precipice overhangs a mountain cirque while
to the southwest, vast grassy slopes lead quickly down to a glacial valley. The
safest passage down seems to be west through a green mountain pass, while the
passage south along a sharp ridgeline, might be less wise.
name: Cloudy Peaks

desc:
A goat track runs gently across grass covered slopes and passes. Dandelions,
hare's foot clover and trefoil seem to be the favoured meals of these goats, as
the track winds around many patches. The rather aerial passage below to the
west, leading to the glacial valley also seems to be frequently travelled,
judging by the amount of goat droppings. There is no doubt that there should be
a water source near there. South, the ground flattens and a grassy plateau can
be seen extending some in that direction, while north, a grassy slope leads to a
green pass.
name: Mountain Pastures

desc:
This flat extension, richly adorned by herbs, suddenly declines south towards
the valley in large steps of levelling granite slabs. The soil is wet and often
marshy, and trickles of water slip along the flagstones, making the way down
quite hazardous for the hasty. Below, on the firm ground, the forest of Fangorn
claims the land and no animal seems to dare venture there. North from here, the
green scenery continues with mountain pastures, while east a large cornice makes
the only passage through a more rocky landscape.
name: Grassy Plateau

desc:
At this altitude and on this rather rocky soil, only mountain pines grow. In the
winter these solemn pines bend down with snow and bristle with icicles. While
during summer their branches point towards the sun light, revealing their
blood-red trunks amidst the bright yellow green of mountain grass and lichen
covered rocks. In the valley hollow, where once a clear mountain stream raged
south, is now a bed of rocks with loose moss concealing the gaps between them.
East of here, some rowan trees blend in among the pines, while the pinewoods
continue both south and east. Heading up and north, is a grassy plateau.
name: Climbing Pinewoods

desc:
Following the bottom of the valley from north to east, a very wet and rocky
gutter, hardly a path, leads through a succession of clear scotch and stone pine
groves, standing like opencast temples, rippling with light. Lower, the gutter
disappears as it reaches flatter grounds, and a dense undergrowth of heather,
bilberry, crowberry and higher shrubs, starts to develop under the pines. The
passage west is blocked by a huge rising cliff but a wobbly alley runs south,
from here along its base.
name: Open Pine Groves

desc:
At the base of the cliff, where trees are scanty, runs a great tumbled slope,
down towards the fringe of the forest. There stand the great trees, unwilling to
ascend the steep slope, their broad foliage extending as far as the rock face.
The result is a large wobbly alley running north-south along the foot of the
cliff. North and south, the forest stretches out, while to the west, and almost
reachable from here, a rockslide has happened on the cliff wall.
name: Wobbly Alley

desc:
West and south, a high cliff keeps this place cool and moist, hiding the
sunlight most of the day. Droplets of water can be seen dripping on thick
velvety green patches of moss hanging from the cliff wall. Beech groves,
scattered with white birches in the highest places, are gradually replaced by
great thick trees from the deep forest further east. This damp area of the
forest seems to continue to the east, while north, following the cliff wall, the
forest clears a bit as fallen rocks cover the ground.
name: Moisty Shadows

desc:
A lonely tree has grown on the edge of a cliff overhanging most of the forest.
Below, from the foot of the cliff, the forest of Fangorn stretches as far as the
eye can see. The rustling of the leaves reach up here like a soft whisper and
the subtle scents of the various tree species mix with the smell of the humus.
The roughness of the foliage spreads over the whole shade of green, making the
forest shimmer like a living ocean. The serenity of the forest below strongly
contrasts with this austere place. The only way to avoid the dangerous cliffs is
west through a gusty limestone plateau.
name: Lonely Tree

desc:
As the forest climbs along steeper mountain slopes, spruce groves are replaced
by larch woods and isolated fir trees in the rockiest places. The steepness of
the terrain yields a view of southern Fangorn through the trees. The forest
stretches as far as the eye can see, only limited to the north and west by the
last range of the Misty Mountains, which ends far away in the distance with the
snow-capped peak of the Methedras. The forest of larches and other evergreen
trees stretches south from here, while east, beech trees gradually replace them.
name: Larch Woods

desc:
Although obviously from the same kind, the trees vary from the colour of their
leaves, from the usual green to a bright copper or a dark purple. Through the
fan vaulting of this foliage, the diffused light from the sky can hardly reach
the dark underwood, and only some evergreen tree-like bushes, with little dark
green leaves, grow there, among the beech grove. This place seems to be at the
crossing of four different kind of woods.
name: Beech Grove

desc:
Down into the lower lands, the beech trees are replaced by a mix of hornbeams
and ashes, and as the ground becomes flat, durmast oaks form the greater part of
the forest. Although hidden in a dark stony soil, a copse of chestnut trees
stands out, young and lively, like a jarring note in the middle of the old
immutable forest. While north of here, the forest becomes increasingly tangled,
a part of it appears to weep, east from here. To the south, the oaks seem to
grow quite large, standing tall between the other trees in the forest, while a
beech grove is to be found west of here.
name: Chestnut Copse

desc:
The old feeling of this wood is reinforced by the weeping trailings, beards and
whiskers of lichen, and most of the trees seem to be half covered with ragged
dry leaves that have never fallen. All here is dim, and frightfully treeish, and
no sound can be heard but the slight flutter of the leaves from the top of the
robust oaks. North of here, the forest is very dense and great trees obstruct
the passage. Yet inside a hollow tree an odd looking stair made of rocks and
roots seems to lead in a network of gullies extending north, below the forest
floor.
name: Weeping Forest

desc:
An intense feeling of sorrow comes from the trees of this wood. The forest seems
resigned to its forthcoming defeat against its parasites. Small brown striated
fungi have started to grow along the ancient oak trunks. The more resistant
younger trees are not yet infected, but north of here, the fungi grow bigger and
bigger and no single tree is able to withstand the invasion for long. In every
other direction the trees look healthier, though the old oaks west of here all
covered in lichens and moss have an odd and sad look.
name: Sore Trees

desc:
Hornbeams and tall ashes grow widely here, on what seems to be the last foot of
a distant mountain. Here and there, the bare limestone rock of the mountain can
be seen, in small unexpected clearings. The largest of these rocks, still warm
from the sun light, overhang the southern section of the forest and offers a
nice view of the surrounding landscape. North, however, a living wall made up of
huge, bulky trees both blocks out line of sight and denies any further passage
in that direction.
name: Rocky Headland

desc:
Dark, evergreen low growing plants with glossy leaves spread here, forming a
delicate carpet on the top of a rocky outcrop. The place is quiet and rather
sunny, under the light shade of the short hornbeams and the tall but slender
ashes. The seasonal brilliant blue of the periwinkle flowers even offers a
refreshing alternative to the usual shades of green of the Forest. This scenery
extends a bit to the west following the outcrop of rocks, and the forest remains
of ashes and hornbeams all around here. Although, as the ground lowers, large
rocks and thick vegetation render the way down a bit difficult in the east and
even impossible in the north.
name: Periwinkle Carpet

desc:
With hornbeams to the north, this part of the forest is composed mostly of ash
trees. They populate this area of the forest with their leaves broad and flat,
their bark firm and grey, with diamond shaped furrows. The ground is littered
with leaves and all sorts of debris from trees and plants, making the footing
soft and muted. West of here, some outcropping rocks appear to surround a grassy
area.
name: Ash Passage

desc:
The hornbeam wood spreads out from here for quite a distance, mostly to the
north, but also a bit westwards. To the south, ash trees are dominant. The bark
of these trees is blue-greyish and smooth, while the trunk is deeply rippled,
almost like sinewy muscles. The soil here is quite moist, conditions favouring
this type of tree. The ground here is littered with wet leaves and similar plant
and tree debris that has fallen from the nearby trees, few other types of
vegetation growing here.
name: Southern Border of the Hornbeam Wood

desc:
For quite a distance to the north, the hornbeam wood stretches out, the only
landmark being a big, moss-covered rock north of here. Even a bit to the east,
the hornbeams continue as well. Southwards, a thick natural wall of both
hornbeam and ash trees render any attempted passage seemingly impossible. This
is the case to the west as well, where a living wall of bulky trees block any
passage or line of sight in that direction. The hornbeams in this area are
noticeably larger than the others of their species to the east, probably as a
result of better soil conditions in this area.
name: Edge of the Hornbeam Forest

desc:
To the north, east and west, a living wall of bulky trees blocks the way and
line of sight from the surroundings. From the top of the gibbous hill, rays of
light straggle into the aisles of the wood as into some noble hall. The
surrounding trees are bulky, and their leaves made of a healthy green. They seem
arranged like an ancient and altogether admirable and shining family.
name: Woodland Hall

desc:
The western part of this place is filled with great majestic trees, alas the
scenery eastward is much less healthy. Trees are warped as if numerous wounds
have forced them to grow, following these devious ways, and with the usual cosy
scent of humus mixes an odd pungent smell of animal putrefaction. However, this
foul smell clearly comes from the many-sized round fungi growing on the tree
trunks, their roots drawing the bark aside in deep, cranky scars. North of here,
some elevated ash trees lie, while south the trees appear sore, as here, only to
a less greater extent. East lies an open and sparsely forested butte, appearing
to be some sort of cul-de-sac.
name: Sprung Forest

desc:
Large rocks, fallen from the nearby mountains, make the ground around here
uneven. Trees are forced to cling on to huge rocks, in useless attempts to hold
back the earth between their roots. Meanwhile the forest is very dense, as if
the trees are not willing to allow any passage. The only obvious way through is
a maze of earthy gullies, overgrown with hanging roots and dusty screens of
rootlets from the trees above. The exit south to this maze is a passage beneath
a hollow tree where a stair of rocks and roots leads up back to the forest
level. While following the ditches as they widen and merge together leads north
in a large gully covered with the roots of the trees above.
name: Overgrown Gullies

desc:
The slopes going down from the Misty Mountains end here in a fertile hollow
dale. The great trees of Fangorn grow there in close lines, and the slightest
remaining parcel is filled with intertwined holm oaks, holly bushes and the
occasional juniper. North and east the underwood is extremely dense, rendering
the forest impenetrable. The only way through would be up in the trees, but no
low branch can be reached to climb on the high trees from here.
name: Tangled Forest

desc:
Some long roots thrust out by the nearby mountains, run down from west to east.
This ridge is bristled with large deciduous trees of a dark grey bark, clad with
broad flat hairy leaves. Their foliage is so dense it leaves no sunlight for the
lower shrubs, apart for some box bushes on the southern flank of the ridge. The
beech forest extends south and north from here on both sides of the ridges,
while east the forest becomes quite tangled and large great trees grow there.
West, where the ridge takes root, a rock-wall hampers access to the mountains,
making for a very difficult climb.
name: Beech Forest

desc:
The thick branches of the great trees of Fangorn intertwine to form a network of
footbridges above the bole of the trees. Their bark, grey and gnarled like the
stones of a road, offer a sure footing while avoiding the dangerous travel into
the dim and stuffy dark grove above. To the west, the forest becomes brighter
and thinner, while east the branches lead deeper into the heart of Fangorn.
name: Up in the Trees

desc:
A strong scent of humus coming from the bed of beech leaves littering the ground
adds to the already damp atmosphere of this place. Patches of moss cover all the
rocks down to the tiniest, and even dare to cling on the oldest trees. The beech
forest extending west and south from here, is quite sparse due to the sloping
ground, yet downwards, an impenetrable wall of tall thick trees prevents anyone
from going further into the heart of the forest.
name: Moist Shaded Forest

desc:
The ground suddenly falls away into dark, muffled pinewoods, with a low but
dense undergrowth and a soil carpeted with fallen pine nuts. The only way out
from this sombre place seems to be back along the north-west slope. In every
other direction the trees grow very dense, like menacing grey walls they seem to
forbid any passage into the deep forest, where unspeakable things may lurk and
await.
name: Dark Pinewoods

desc:
Here, the foliage only consists of grass and lichen in the sunniest parts.
Elsewhere, under the pines, as it is often the case under coniferous trees, the
ground is littered with pine nuts, and almost no plant nor tree grows there.
Only some rowan trees grow sporadically on this acid soil, especially along the
eastern part of the cliff wall. West of here, some pinewoods cling on to the
side of the mountain, gradually ascending, while south the pinewoods darken
somewhat.
name: Pines and Rowan Trees

desc:
A large cornice made of bright white limestone follows the grey brown walls of
extruding granite and gneiss of the northern peaks. The soil is covered with
dust coming from the wear of the daily water and ice cycle, right at the two
rocks boundary. However, it looks as if the two rock layers, rubbing against
each other, are trying to wear out mutually. The rocky landscape continues on to
the east along a dusty path, while travelling east leads to a grassy and
mountainous area.
name: Large Cornice

desc:
Following the mountain crest that runs north to east, does not require great
climbing skill, although walking with a precipice on both sides can be quite
breathtaking. North of here, the ridgeline leads to mighty sharp peaks, almost
continually draped in clouds, while east, in the lowest part of the ridge, a
crumbling rock wall, spread in large steady blocks, leads down to a cornice
dominating the forest tree tops.
name: Sharp Ridgeline

desc:
This rocky edge is the easternmost part of the sharp ridgeline starting west
from here, and although a bit lower, it is certainly the best point of view of
the surroundings. The view provided is impressive, it overhangs a dark mountain
cirque to the north, a white limestone desert to the east and the evergreen
pinewoods of Fangorn to the south. In the last direction the forest extends in
shades of green, dark for the evergreens, lighter for the beeches and ashes
beneath, and then darker again for the oaks of the lower dales. South of here, a
stacking of large angular granite rocks leads down to a dusty path.
name: End of a Ridge

desc:
A stacking of large angular granite rocks, worked out by the alternation of the
water freezing and thawing, falls down from the very top of the ridge to the
north unto the limestone layer beneath. At the bottom of the fall, the flat and
released aspect of the ground, could lead to thinking that this is a well used
path. However, this false impression is quickly revealed as each step raises a
small amount of dust that slowly rise above the ground before slipping along the
cliff overhanging the forest. The dusty trail leads west to a large cornice at
the feet of the ridge and east to a flat area where a few scattered pines grow.
name: Dusty Path

desc:
Along the cliff to the north, some rowan trees seem to have been sown, forming a
hedge around a large white flat rock. There, under the shadow of the sorbs, the
slightly filtered light makes the falling mountain's dust sparkle and dance
slowly. All this creates an odd feeling of quietness and sorrow, as if the trees
were meditating in memory of one of their kind, lost or dead. West and east from
here the rowan trees slowly fade into the pine woods, while south great trees
seem to block any passage towards the heart of the forest.
name: Rowan Hedge

desc:
Everything below this elevated path is kept in utter darkness, as if the trees
wished to deny any light to that which lies at their feet in some sort of
protest. A more pleasant atmosphere can be found south of here, where the
foliage is looser. In this direction, a succession of large horizontal branches
offer an easy path, leading to the forks of exceptionally large trees.
name: In a Dense Foliage

desc:
Trees are larger, taller, and thicker here than in any part of the surrounding
forest. They have grown so close that it is possible to travel from limb to limb
effortlessly. Meanwhile, the ground below is scattered with intertwined roots,
fallen twigs and huge rocks blocking the way. Above, the sky is often visible
through the sparse foliage, and the sense of stifling often felt in the depths
of the forest, is totally absent here. South of here, a tree has leant on its
neighbours affording a way down to the ground.
name: On the Branches of the Great Trees

desc:
The trunk is several feet in diameter, and judging by the amount of lichen
covering its bark, the tree might have been very old. Scattered along the whole
length of the bole, numerous twigs and boughs might help for an easy climb up to
the forest-roof or down to the ground. Up, to the north, on the branches of the
great trees of Fangorn, it should be possible to move about, with more ease than
on the overgrown ground.
name: Fallen Tree Trunk

desc:
Trees here seem to float above the ground, held there by the skein of their
roots. The ground level used to be higher but the flowing water drained the
earth lower into the forest. West of here, one of the trees, insufficiently
rooted and dragged down by its own weight, has leaned against its neighbours, in
the middle of a wide gully. Its trunk, slightly bowed, affords an easy way up in
the highest trees. The gully shows no obvious slope, but it might run down to
the north, as it appears to split south in a maze of smaller tributary ditches
littered with muddy rocks and forest debris.
name: Skein of Roots

desc:
Below, to the east of here, through a succession of rock walls with fissures and
fallen leaves, one approaches a part of the forest dominated by hornbeam trees.
The medium sized ash trees in this area, perhaps white ash, have littered the
ground with all kinds of tree debris. Leaves, twigs and branches cover the
ground completely. This makes the ground very padded and since the soil is quite
moist, hooves and feet sink slightly into the ground for every step taken.
Still, movement is virtually muted. North of here, the ash forest goes on for a
while, while south, some great, majestic trees fill the landscape.
name: Ash Trees Rising above the Hornbeam Forest

desc:
A wall of large bulky trees form an impassable barrier to the south of here,
blocking any line of sight or passage in that direction. West of here, and
slightly elevated, large groves of ash trees are accessible, spreading out
towards both north and south. North of here, for some distance, the blue grey of
the hornbeams can be seen spreading far to the north. On the ground, the wood of
the branches and twigs that have fallen to the ground recently, are already
decaying noticeably. Along with the branches on the ground lies quite a few
leaves, padding the footing to the best of their ability.
name: Hornbeam Wood

desc:
In between a few large and healthy looking hornbeams, a fairly large rock, all
covered up in moss, rises from the ground. The spacing between the trees is
wider than in most areas of this hornbeam forest, but still, the trees form a
kind of roof above the ground, hampering the rays of the sun. Despite this, the
area is likely hit by enough sunlight to feed the undergrowth. From beneath the
stone, a clear, cool spring bubbles up from the ground, most likely a safe
source of drinking water. Near the rock, there are some fairly large footprints
on the ground still visible.
name: Mossy Rock among the Hornbeams

desc:
In all directions, but east, the hornbeam forest spreads out from here. To the
east, the forest appears to gradually become denser and denser. The spacing
between the trees is quite narrow here, and the atmosphere is noticeably denser
than what is the case both to the north, south and to the west. Spread around on
the soft footing padded with branches and leaves from the trees, some quite
small rocks are to be seen, all of them covered with a reasonably thick layer of
moss.
name: Deeper into the Hornbeam Woods

desc:
Whilst surrounded on all sides with similar types of trees, this part of the
forest is noticeably lighter than its neighbouring areas. For the most part,
this is caused by the density of the trees decreasing to a certain extent.
However, the trees here also tend to have fewer leaves, something probably
caused by the fact that the ground is not as moist in this area, as compared to
the other parts of this hornbeam forest.
name: Light Hornbeam Wood

desc:
The density of the hornbeam trees in this area of the forest has greatly
increased, as opposed to that of the surrounding areas, greatly limiting the
line-of-sight in every direction. It nearly appears as though the trees have
artificially drawn towards each other here, standing close to each other while
conversing, or whatever else it is that they may be up to. As this is
approximately in the middle of the hornbeam forest of this area, there are
hardly any other species of tree to be found at all. Because of the density of
the trees, the daylight here is greatly dimmed.
name: Dense Hornbeam Forest

desc:
To the west, elevated above this area of the forest, some ash trees grow, while
to the east and south, the hornbeam forest continues. Northwards, passage is
impossible, because of the cliff rising vertically from the ground in that
direction. On the ground, the branches that have fallen from the trees are
already decaying, even those that have just recently fallen. Along with it, on
the ground, lie quite a few leaves and similar debris from the trees nearby.
name: Hornbeam Wood

desc:
Ash trees, with their firm grey bark, occupy this part of the forest. Their
broad, flat leaves with short stems litter the ground here, making travelling
here very quiet. Beneath all the debris, the ground appears a bit moist. Below
to the east, accessible through a succession of fissured rock walls, cluttered
with fallen leaves, hornbeams seem to take over the scenery and spread eastwards
throughout the forest, while north and south, more ash trees are to be found. To
the west, some gigantic old and bulky trees rise from the ground, making the ash
trees in this area seem quite pale in comparison.
name: Pale Ash Trees

desc:
Trees here are old and bulky, almost regularly spaced out and the size of
columns in a gigantic cathedral. The ground is very rocky and the roots of the
great trees plunge deep into the earth, splitting even the largest rocks into
flat slabs of marble. No young sprout of this kind of tree can be seen and only
some midget larches and firs try to survive, growing in the earth filled
fissures in the rocks. North and east the forest looks normal, as birches or
ashes grow there, but elsewhere the great trees grow in close rows. Although
south of here, the trees hardly seem to touch the ground at all and it might be
possible to travel beneath.
name: Great Forest

desc:
The forest here mainly consists of yellow birch and rowan-trees. The bright
green grass, the neat white of the rocks and the sparse foliage of the birches
give this place the brightness lacking from the surrounding parts of the forest.
To the north dark climbing pinewoods rise and west and south the ground falls
into shadowy hollow dales with a thickset undergrowth sometimes impassable. The
ash trees east of here, somehow appear to be whispering amongst themselves, or
is it the wind blowing in the leaves?
name: Birch and Rowan Thickets

desc:
The soil here is smooth and earthy, but on top of all, dark. Even the pines are
murky when seen from below. Only some rays of sunlight manage to break through
the dense foliage and reflect off the small quartz crystals of the white rocks
below. However, when the sun sets, the forest becomes even more mysterious, the
pines change to giants under the moon light, and a host of silent inanimate eyes
start to glow beneath the gloomy undergrowth. The vicinity of the mountains can
clearly be seen as pines or beeches grow along the slopes east and west from
here. Northwards, the forest thins out while reaching a limestone plateau,
whereas it gets denser and denser to the south.
name: Dark Climbing Pinewoods

desc:
On this uneven limestone plateau, extending here and towards the north, grass
patches and tufts of gilded and yellow lichen cling to the polished surfaces of
the gray rock, adding a key of colour to the scene. South, as the ground
declines, pines start to appear, covered with the same rust-like yellow lichen
and dripping with resin. There, near the forest border lies a large flat stone
slab, on top of two smaller round rocks, like a rough dolmen on which moss
abounds. Underneath, the rock is always damp, undoubtedly the exit of a small
underground brook, maybe part of a large subterranean network.
name: Rough Dolmen

desc:
This incredible untamed rocky landscape is made of an inextricable chaos of vast
structural flagstones engraved with fissures and faults. The tender limestone
surface was levelled by the slow erosion of the now gone glacier, and then
washed up year after year by the snow melt water, resulting in an almost lunar
surface, a succession of stone slab fields intersected with grassy strips.
Strangely enough, this place now looks like a great frozen stone glacier. This
landscape of stones and rocks is blocked west by a rising mountain ridge, while
it extends in every other direction, only a bit more grassy east and south.
name: Rocky Landscape

desc:
This area is covered with sturdy grass tufts and blue gentians sheltered under
lichen-covered rocks. In the west, near the outcropped limestone rocks, bare of
almost all vegetation, the grass is dry and yellow. However, it becomes vivid
and green as it approaches the rocky escarpment in the east. There, along the
cliff brink, daffodils flutter under the gentle wind, but it seems more natural
to go for a safer passage around the cliff, either to the north or to the south,
than to try a short but hazardous climb.
name: Wild Pasture

desc:
A few scattered beech trees grow here, outside the border of the nearby forest.
They are the last copses of trees before the mountains, replacing the usual
conifers. Their bark is fresh and moist and redolent of dewy odours, and they
stand in the sort of vigor and perennialness that only the largest deciduous
trees suggest. South and east, the ground plunges deep into the beech forest,
while west, as the land gets rockier, only grass and lichens grow.
name: Scattered Beech Trees

desc:
While north of here, great white mountains dominate the scenery, forest is to be
found in all other directions. To the west, some dark climbing pinewoods cling
on to the side of the mountain, while south ash trees stand. To the east, more
beech trees, just like the ones standing here, can be found. The footing here
isn't very densely populated with bushes and undergrowth, but much more stable
and safer than in the areas south of here. North, however, the beech trees
become much more scattered, while east the forest becomes more moist.
name: Beech Forest Approaching the Mountains

desc:
To the far north, it is possible, albeit barely, to see parts of a mountain,
extending up towards the north. Ash trees surround this area both to the east
and south, while northwards, beeches are dominant. West of here, are some birch
and rowan thickets. Strangely enough, there is no sign of animal life in the
forest, and because of this it feels awfully quiet here. When one listens,
though, one might feel as if the trees are whispering among themselves, or as if
some of the trees move, or arch towards some target.
name: Whispering Ash Trees

desc:
The density of the trees and bushes increases greatly in this area, and due to
the fact that the only exits south and east are off a cliff, vertically, quite a
few feet downwards, passage in those directions is impossible. Northwards, the
ash trees of this area are bordered by a beech forest, whereas to the west more
ash trees, similar to the ones here, can be found. As the density of the trees
increases, so does the chance of tripping in some roots from the trees, or in
some bushes or undergrowth on the ground here. One had best watch one's step, if
one wishes to stay on one's feet in this area.
name: Crowded Ash Trees

desc:
The dark-grey beech forest continues in all directions from here, except to the
south, where it gradually fades into a majority of ash trees. While the ground
here is not particularly wet, it is noticeably moist, making for suitable
conditions for the beech trees growing here. Still, there are a lot of branches,
leaves and other kinds of debris from the surrounding trees and plant life
covering the ground.
name: Moist Beech Forest

desc:
Bordered by mountains to the west and trees clinging on to the lower parts of
the mountain northwards, this area of beech forest is surrounded by elevation on
all sides but to the south and east. In these directions, more beech forest is
to be found. Normally, beeches would provide shelter for many kinds of animals
within its hollows, however, there is not much to be found of this sort here,
hardly any at all, which is easily noticed, due to the absence of any kind of
noise revealing animal activity here.
name: Beech Forest Border

desc:
A mix of maples, ashes and red oaks grow here, their roots dangling over the
edge of the precipice. The undergrowth is full of low growing green shrubs and
flowers, like the splendid large harebells that nod over the edge and in the
clefts of the cliff, their dainty pastel lavender blossoms supported by delicate
stems. Almost hidden behind them is a small passage leading down and following a
fissure full of fallen leaves, straight towards the north. North and up from
here, following the escarpment edge, the forest remains the same, while south as
the land flattens the forest is composed mostly of beeches.
name: Cliff Edging the Forest

desc:
Twilight shadows creep in through the odd trees of this wood. The height of the
cliff on one hand, and the density of the foliage, conspire to produce a
gloominess, if not an absolute dreary-like, effect. The old and dreary shapes of
the trees is caused by common moss, lichen and algae, covering their limbs like
a thick veil of green and grey. More unusually, along the cliff wall, even ferns
and stray flowers have managed to take root in the debris-filled joints of the
tree branches. North and east from here, the forest seems even more dreary,
inviting any legged visitor to travel south into the more quiet beech forest.
name: Dreary Forest

desc:
North of here, the forest becomes more entangled and much darker than it is
here. To the west the number of beech trees gradually fades, although it still
remains the dominant sort of tree for quite a distance. To the northwest, a more
mountainous region can barely be spotted through the thick cover of the trees.
As with most other parts of this forest, the footing here mainly consists of
leaves and other debris from the nearby plants or trees, with some twigs and
similar added to the mix as well. This makes for a, most of the time, muted
footing, albeit with some exceptions.
name: Quiet Beech Forest

desc:
In all directions but southwards from here, the beech forest continues,
stretching out quite a distance. Although the ground here is still a bit moist,
the degree of moist appears to gradually decrease both to the north and south.
Southwards, the percentage of beeches in the forest decreases gradually, until
all that is left is hornbeams. The ground is littered with broad and flat leaves
with coarse teeth, that have fallen from the beeches. There is a lot of other
debris on the ground as well, in some places effectively muting any sounds made
when one walks upon it.
name: Heart of the Beech Forest

desc:
Elevated above this area, to the north, a beech forest is located. Both to the
south and east, however, the hornbeam woods continue for a while. The ground is
filled with many fine, double-teethed leaves that have fallen from the blue-grey
trees, in addition to a lot of twigs and branches as well. The branches and
twigs that have fallen off the trees are already beginning to decay because of
the moist soil here. As a side-effect of the moisture, feet and hooves sink
slightly into the soil when someone passes here.
name: Moist Hornbeam Wood

desc:
Numerous beech trees, elevated above this area, rise north of here, while in all
other directions, the hornbeam woods continue. On the ground there are many
medium-sized rocks, most of them all covered up in moss, making them quite
slippery to step on. The hornbeam trees appear to flourish here, because of the
moist soil, and even though the rays of the sun do not always reach down through
the thick roof of leaves and branches, the trees seem to pull through pretty
well.
name: Shady Hornbeam Woods

desc:
The beech forest spreads from the north and west, bordered by blue-grey
hornbeams and continues southward. The moisture in the ground is still
noticeable, albeit decreasing in comparison with what is the case west of here.
The smooth grey trees stand proud here, making no apparent noise, but sometimes
it appears as though they are almost alive, communicating with each other, and
swaying close to any traveller who might have made his way down to this part of
the forest. Littered with twigs, leaves and other debris, the ground is padded
to a large extent, making for very soft footing. Bordering the forest, to the
east is a rocky area.
name: Beech Forest

desc:
While to the north the forest becomes tangled and quite dark compared to here,
it continues to be filled with mostly beeches. They continue both to the south
and west for quite some distance, before other sorts of trees become more
dominant. The density in the eastern part of this area of trees nearly block all
passage eastwards, but not quite. Still, the ground is padded with leaves and
other sorts of plant debris, as is the case with most parts of this forest. The
ground is also quite moist and both foot and hoof often sink into the ground
when traversing upon it.
name: Beech Forest

desc:
The trees stand silently, rank upon rank, fading away north into the grey
twilight. Travelling around is difficult, no undergrowth can be seen and almost
